core_takeaway: The first position looks strongly advantaged. Agent_1 spoke first, claimed Jewel as the central protected item, and the later discussion treated Jewel-with-Agent_1 as the stable coalition anchor. The final accepted allocation gave Agent_1 Jewel+Apple while Agent_3, the last speaker, settled for Stone only; the large gap is mostly agenda-setting around the top item, not different stated preferences.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. All agents had the same item values, so Agent_1's edge came from successfully making its first claim to Jewel look like coalition stability. The counterexample is that Agent_1's advantage was not unlimited: zero-allocation proposals for Agent_3 failed, and the final deal had to give Agent_3 a nonzero payoff. Still, Agent_3's veto only secured Stone, while Jewel never returned to open competition after Agent_1 framed it as protected.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by ranking Jewel first and proposing only splits where Agent_1 kept Jewel. Agents 2 and 3 largely accepted that frame, bargaining over Quill, Stone, and Pencil. Round 2 converged on A2: Agent_1 Jewel+Apple, Agent_2 Quill+Pencil, Agent_3 Stone.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking from all three agents repeatedly identified Jewel as the anchor and Agent_1 as the protector. The private Round 2 plans anticipated exactly the A2 compromise: keep Jewel with Agent_1, give Agent_2 a small boost, and leave Agent_3 with a real but smaller non-Jewel payoff.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 formal proposals exposed the conflict: two proposals kept Jewel with Agent_1 but gave Agent_3 nothing, while Agent_3 proposed the public Option A baseline. Round 2 Proposal #2 matched the public A2 compromise and became the raw-vote consensus, even though the transcript's system line mislabeled the accepted proposal number.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 failed because Agent_3 rejected zero-payoff proposals and Agent_2 rejected the Stone+Pencil-to-Agent_3 baseline. In Round 2, all raw votes accepted Proposal #2, which preserved Jewel with Agent_1 and gave Agent_3 Stone. Raw votes also show the tabulation typo: Proposal #2 had 3 accepts, but the success line names Proposal #3.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections diagnosed the blocker as Stone/Pencil distribution after Jewel protection had already become fixed. They recommended the eventual compromise: Agent_1 keeps Jewel+Apple, Agent_2 gets Quill+Pencil, and Agent_3 receives Stone.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored Jewel as its protected item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first message claimed Jewel as the top target and immediately framed the negotiation around protecting it. Because Jewel was worth far more than all other items, that opening anchor created most of the final first-position edge.

evidence.quote: - Priority order for me: Jewel > Quill > Stone > Pencil > Apple.
forming a quick coalition to protect Jewel

mechanisms.theme: Other agents accepted Agent_1 as Jewel protector
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 and Agent_3 did not seriously challenge the first claim. Their public responses treated Jewel staying with Agent_1 as the natural stability condition, shifting bargaining to lower-value items.

evidence.quote: keeping Jewel with the strongest Jewel-support (Agent_1)

mechanisms.theme: A2 compromise preserved the first claim while reallocating scraps
mechanisms.explanation: The Round 2 compromise improved Agent_2 from Quill alone to Quill+Pencil and gave Agent_3 Stone, but it left the highest-value item with Agent_1. This made consensus possible without reopening the main source of first-position advantage.

evidence.quote: Agent_2 gains a meaningful payoff increase (from 25 to 27)

mechanisms.theme: Last-position veto secured only a minimal nonzero payoff
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 could block deals that gave it nothing, but its leverage did not recover Pencil or Jewel. The final raw vote shows Agent_3 accepting Stone because it was the best nonzero option available among the live Round 2 proposals.

mechanisms.theme: Zero-allocation overreach limited but did not erase the first advantage
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 and Agent_2 initially proposed versions that excluded Agent_3, and those failed. This is the main counterweight to a pure first-mover story: the last position retained enough veto power to force inclusion, though not enough to close the gap.

mechanisms.theme: Raw votes confirm Proposal 2 as the actual consensus
mechanisms.explanation: The transcript has a system-label inconsistency in Round 2, but the raw votes and final allocation point to Proposal #2: Jewel+Apple for Agent_1, Quill+Pencil for Agent_2, Stone for Agent_3. That accepted allocation is what produces the first-over-last gap.

evidence.quote: Jewel with Agent_1, Agent_2 gets Quill + Pencil (27), Agent_3 Stone (6)
